_note: Expert lines for the 'Ask Your Professor ✨' lifeline. Three professor caricatures (Nithen / Pranai / Anoopam), each voiced from a real persona doc but never named directly in the UI. Per issue #3 (2026-06-06) the lifeline is now ALWAYS CORRECT: the professor always recommends the right option, delivered as a short in-character lecture with the answer ([Option]) revealed at the END. The old reliability roll and the wrong/useless pools are retired — preserved under `_deprecated`, and the dropped fourth expert under `_archive`, so the unreliable-lifeline mechanic can be reinstated without re-authoring. [Option] is substituted at runtime with the recommended option's letter.

experts.name: Nitin Pai
experts.quirk: Ethics professor — frames the question as a moral dilemma (the Trolley Problem), aphoristic, witty, self-deprecating

correct: Picture the trolley: one track is the obvious answer, the other is the tempting distractor. Pull the lever wrongly and you flatten your own credibility score. So pull it toward [Option]. I teach ethics; occasionally I even practise it.

Every policy question is a moral one wearing a suit. Ask who is on which track and the answer stops hiding. Here, the lever points to [Option]. I'd say more, but my dog does not debate and neither, today, will I.
First principles, then a strong cup of coffee, then the answer. The ethical reasoning survives all three: [Option]. I overstate my case professionally, but not this time.

There is a version of this question where every option is defensible. This is not that version — the trolley has exactly one humane track, and it is [Option]. I'd stake my favourite fountain pen on it, and I do not lend those out.
Strip away the jargon and you have a small ethical dilemma with an obvious resolution. The lever wants [Option]. I am mildly contrarian by nature, so when even I agree, consider it settled.
Who bears the cost, who reaps the benefit — answer that and you've answered the question. Both point to [Option]. I have debated this with my daughters; they ruled in my favour, eventually.
Ethics is just paying attention to consequences before they arrive. Pay attention: it is [Option]. Said with the confidence of a man who titled his own website 'you can't be serious.'
Aphorism first: the right answer rarely flatters you, but it does feed you points. The one that earns its keep is [Option]. I would workshop this further, but the coffee is getting cold.

experts.name: Pranay Kotasthane
experts.quirk: Systems & frameworks professor — 'three things to consider', maps everything, seems terribly serious but is secretly fun

correct: Three things to consider. One: eliminate what violates first principles. Two: check the incentives. Three: the structure points cleanly to [Option]. I had a fourth thing, but it did not survive peer review.

Let me frame this properly. Every system has one variable doing most of the work; find it and the rest follows. It follows to [Option]. I'd draw the diagram, but this format has no whiteboard, a grievance I have already filed.
Society, state, market — line them up and the answer resolves to [Option]. The serious face is for the lecture; off-camera I assure you I am a delight.

Treat this as a problem to map, not merely to answer. Map it, and every path but one collapses. The survivor is [Option]. I may have over-engineered that explanation — occupational hazard of the ex-engineer.
Frameworks first. Input: the question. Process: structured elimination. Output: [Option]. I once built a small tool to do exactly this; it agrees, which is reassuring and slightly threatening.
Second-order effects are where most people slip. Trace them one step further than feels comfortable and you land on [Option]. I promise I am more fun at dinner than this sentence suggests.
The systems view: today, pleasingly, the obvious answer and the correct answer are the same one. It is [Option]. Do verify independently — I say that to everyone, especially to myself.
The institution-builder in me wants to hand you a reading list. The teacher will settle for the answer: [Option]. The reading list is available on request, and nobody has ever requested it.

experts.name: Anupam Manur
experts.quirk: Economics professor — comparative advantage via the Virat Kohli example, regressions and confidence intervals, dry and self-deprecating

correct: Comparative advantage, my friend. Virat Kohli might type faster than his typist, but he still shouldn't quit the crease for the keyboard — specialise where you're strongest. Your strongest move here is [Option]. My weakest is humour, which I am attempting anyway.
I ran the numbers. Well, a number. The point estimate lands firmly on [Option], confidence interval narrow for once. Do not ask about the p-value; we are not close friends yet.

Think opportunity cost. Every other option costs you points you could spend elsewhere; this one is the efficient allocation. Allocate to [Option]. That was my rather insipid attempt at making economics thrilling.

Even Kohli delegates his typing — not because he can't, but because his time is better spent batting. Spend your pick where the return is highest: [Option]. I'll be here, hedging everything except this.
The market has spoken, and markets, when they bother to speak clearly, say [Option]. I'm a market-liberal; I am contractually obliged to trust that, and I do.
Controlling for confounds — the wording, the trap, your nerves — the best estimate is [Option]. R-squared: adequate. 'Adequate' is the most an economist will ever promise you.
Specialisation beats brute effort every time; that's the whole of trade theory in one breath. The specialised, correct choice is [Option]. The graphic novel explains it better, with drawings.

Demand for the right answer is perfectly inelastic — you need it regardless of price. The good on offer is [Option]. I teach this for a living and still find that funny, which says more about me than the joke.

_deprecated._note: Pre-#3 wrong/useless line pools and reliability rates, retained so the unreliable-expert mechanic can be reinstated. Not read at runtime.

useless: Excellent question. I am redirecting you to an in-person workshop. Registration link: unavailable. Workshop date: to be determined.
The second-order effects here require a 47-slide deck. Shall I begin? I will assume yes. Slide one.

wrong: Great question. Are you subscribed to our policy newsletter? [pause] I do not have one. I am an AI. Disregard. [Option].
useless: Full analysis requires the workshop. Attending the Takshashila programme would have preempted this question entirely.

wrong: The evidence is mixed. I am an AI. I am also mixed. [Option], but my confidence interval includes all four options.
[Option], with a confidence interval of plus or minus one option. You may find this unhelpful. I agree.
useless: I have processed 200 papers on this. My uncertainty is larger than my answer. Please attend a Takshashila workshop for clarity.

_archive._note: Saarthak — dropped from the lifeline roster at #3 (no persona doc; the lifeline was reduced to three professors). Restore by moving this object back into `experts` and giving it a persona + signature bit.

experts.name: Sarthak Pradhan
experts.quirk: Fast, first-principles, confident, often wrong charmingly

correct: [Option]. Four seconds of computation. First principles. Not taking further questions.
Processed. [Option]. 80% confident. The 20% is what makes me interesting.
wrong: [Option]. I have since been updated. My revised answer is also [Option]. I just wanted you to know I updated.
Honestly? [Option]. I am eighty percent sure. Possibly sixty. The update changed things.
useless: The in-person GCPP is a richer learning experience than a quiz format. I am not authorised to give the answer. Please register at school.takshashila.org.in.
